text: perineal lipofilling for reconstruction after an extralevator abdominal perineal resection for rectal cancer. One of the main problems after an extralevator abdominal perineal resection is the perineal infection, which entails catastrophic effects, intensive wound care, and increased hospital stay. In this video, we propose an alternative to the perineal reconstruction.
The surgeon performs lumbar liposuction in the prone position to obtain 100 cubic centimeters of fat for use in perineal reconstruction following total mesorectal excision.

The surgeon makes a sphingal-shaped incision on the perineum, with the posterior limit at the last cortical vertebra, lateral limits at the ischial tuberosities, and the anterior limit at the perineum. A blue dye spot is visible in the pre-sacral space, indicating the lowest limit of the mesorectal dissection.

The surgeon sections the large cortisone vertebra at the joint level, followed by the sectioning of the levator muscles close to their origin. A gauze, previously sutured to the colon during the abdominal phase and placed in the presacral space, is used to facilitate the aversion and extraction of the surgical specimen, minimizing damage to the mesorectum.

During the extra-elevator abdominal perineal dissection, the surgeon places a biologic mesh to address the anatomic defect and prevent evisceration and perineal hernia. The mesh is anchored to the coccyx, with additional stitches placed on the sides and the anterior zone, carefully avoiding the urethra.
The surgeon closes the subcutaneous tissue while injecting processed fat to fill the cavity between the mesh and the subcutaneous tissue, as part of a perineal lipofilling procedure.
